---
title: "How this course works"
---

Everything for this course lives on **this website**: the schedule, the slides,
the written notes, and the practicals. {{< meta course.lms >}} is used only for
the things a website cannot do — the login password, announcements, and handing
in work.

Read this page once at the start of the semester. It does not change.

*Why the course is built this way is written up separately, at
[teaching.hoelzer.science](https://teaching.hoelzer.science/philosophy).*

## Getting in

The site is password-protected. There is one shared login for the whole course,
and the username and password are posted in **{{< meta course.lms >}}**.

Your browser will remember it, so you normally type it only once.

::: {.callout-note}
Please do not pass the login outside the course. Some material is usable in
teaching only because access is limited to enrolled participants.
:::

## The website is always current

If I fix a mistake in a slide or a practical, the correction is live for
everyone immediately. When in doubt, reload the page rather than trusting a copy
you saved earlier.

Material is published **as we go**: a session appears in the week we cover it,
and the practicals are posted as soon as we begin working on them. The
[schedule](schedule.qmd) shows what is coming; a session you cannot open yet
simply has not been released.

## Slides

The slides are **interactive HTML**, not PowerPoint or PDF:

- Navigate with the **arrow keys**, or swipe on a tablet.
- Press <kbd>O</kbd> for an overview of all slides; <kbd>Esc</kbd> to leave it.
- Press <kbd>F</kbd> for fullscreen.
- Some code examples highlight line by line as we step through them.

### If you want PDF slides

There is no separate PDF to download, but you can make one yourself from any
deck:

1. Open the slides in **Chrome or Edge** (the export is most reliable there).
2. Open the slide menu (the icon at the **bottom-left**) → **Tools** →
   **PDF Export Mode**.
3. Print with <kbd>Cmd</kbd>/<kbd>Ctrl</kbd> + <kbd>P</kbd> and choose
   **Save as PDF**.

## Notes

Most lectures also have a **Notes** page. The slides are deliberately sparse —
the notes are the complete written version, with the full explanations,
definitions and references. Use the slides to follow along in class and the
notes to revise afterwards.

## Practicals

The practicals are usually small, self-contained programming tasks. Each one
comes with starter code and tests you run yourself, so you always know when a
task is done.

The software you need is pinned, so it installs the same way for everyone. We
can use [pixi](https://pixi.sh): install it, then run `pixi install` once in the
practical repository. We might also use other tools for installing software and
handling dependencies, such as
[Mamba](https://mamba.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) or
[Docker](https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop/).

::: {.callout-warning}
## Windows

Some of the tools are not available for native Windows. If you are on Windows,
work inside **WSL2** (Windows Subsystem for Linux). We will set this up together
in the first practical — do not worry about it before then.
:::

## Where to find what

| You want… | Look in… |
|---|---|
| Slides, notes, practicals, schedule | this website |
| The login password | {{< meta course.lms >}} |
| Announcements and deadlines | {{< meta course.lms >}} |
| Handing in practical work | {{< meta course.lms >}} |

## Getting help

Ask questions in class or in the practical — if one person is stuck, usually
several are. Outside class, reach me at
[{{< meta course.contact >}}](mailto:{{< meta course.contact >}}).
